programs.name: General Strength & Conditioning
programs.description: A four-phase progressive program designed to build movement quality, muscle, strength, and cardiovascular fitness across 28+ weeks. Phase 1 (Foundation, 6 wks) establishes movement patterns and connective tissue resilience. Phase 2 (Hypertrophy, 10 wks) accumulates volume for muscle growth. Phase 3 (Strength, 6 wks) converts mass to neural strength. Phase 4 (Conditioning, 6 wks) peaks VO₂ max and cardiovascular fitness. All plans use Dynamic Equipment — at workout start the app resolves each exercise to the best alternative for your available equipment automatically.
programs.targetProfile: General population — beginner to intermediate, home or commercial gym

plans.name: 4-Day | Foundation
plans.goal: Establish movement quality and work capacity before adding serious load. Sets are 10–15 reps at moderate weight — keep 2–3 reps in reserve on everything. Prioritise technique and consistency over pushing numbers. This base makes every phase that follows safer and more effective.

plans.name: 3-Day | Foundation
plans.goal: Establish movement quality and work capacity before adding serious load. Sets are 10–15 reps at moderate weight — keep 2–3 reps in reserve on everything. Prioritise technique and consistency over pushing numbers. This base makes every phase that follows safer and more effective.

plans.name: 4-Day | Hypertrophy

exercises.notes: Mesocycle 1: 3×10–15 / Mesocycle 2: 4×8–12

plans.goal: Accumulate enough volume and mechanical stress to drive muscle growth. 6–15 reps with progressive overload each session — add weight or reps before moving up. Keep 1–2 reps in reserve. Expect soreness early in the block; that's normal. By week 8 you should be hitting new rep-range PRs.

plans.name: 3-Day | Hypertrophy
plans.goal: Accumulate enough volume and mechanical stress to drive muscle growth. 6–15 reps with progressive overload each session — add weight or reps before moving up. Keep 1–2 reps in reserve. Expect soreness early in the block; that's normal. By week 8 you should be hitting new rep-range PRs.

plans.name: 4-Day | Strength

exercises.notes: Heavy compound — prioritize depth and bracing
Heaviest session of the week

plans.goal: Convert hypertrophy gains into peak force output. Lower reps (3–6), heavier loads, close to technical failure on the last set. Rest 2–3 min between sets — that rest is part of the program. Numbers should climb weekly; if they stall two sessions in a row, drop 10 % and rebuild.

plans.name: 3-Day | Strength
plans.goal: Convert hypertrophy gains into peak force output. Lower reps (3–6), heavier loads, close to technical failure on the last set. Rest 2–3 min between sets — that rest is part of the program. Numbers should climb weekly; if they stall two sessions in a row, drop 10 % and rebuild.

plans.name: 4-Day | Conditioning

exercises.notes: Maintenance — moderate load
40–45 min at conversational pace, 65–75% max HR. Prefer cycling or rowing.
4 rounds × 4 min at 90–95% max HR. 3 min active recovery between rounds. Warm up 10 min, cool down 5 min. Prefer bike or rower.

plans.goal: Shift emphasis toward metabolic fitness and cardiovascular capacity alongside strength maintenance. Push hard on conditioning circuits and finishers — these are meant to be uncomfortable. Keep strength sets controlled at 1–2 RIR. The goal is to be strong AND able to sustain effort.

plans.name: 3-Day | Conditioning

exercises.notes: Maintenance lifting — 2–3 sets × 10–12 reps
40–45 min at conversational pace, 65–75% max HR.
4 rounds × 4 min at 90–95% max HR. 3 min active recovery. Warm up 10 min, cool down 5 min.

plans.goal: Shift emphasis toward metabolic fitness and cardiovascular capacity alongside strength maintenance. Push hard on conditioning circuits and finishers — these are meant to be uncomfortable. Keep strength sets controlled at 1–2 RIR. The goal is to be strong AND able to sustain effort.

mobilityRoutines.name: Morning CARs
mobilityRoutines.notes: Do daily — before coffee if possible. CARs (Controlled Articular Rotations) signal the nervous system that you own full joint range. Daily repetition is the mechanism; skipping days reduces the adaptation signal.

exercises.name: Hip CARs
exercises.notes: Standing, one hand on wall for balance. Maximise the circle — reach forward, out, back, and through. Full tension throughout.

exercises.name: Shoulder CARs
exercises.notes: Keep the rest of the body still. Reach long through the full arc. Critical for climbers — maintains capsular health and scapular dissociation.

exercises.name: Thoracic Spine CARs
exercises.notes: Seated or quadruped. Isolate the T-spine — keep the lumbar quiet. Desk work kills thoracic extension; this is the daily antidote.

exercises.name: Wrist CARs
exercises.notes: Both wrists together or one at a time. Maximise the circle in both directions. Underused but essential for climbers — prepares the wrist joint before any gripping or loading.

exercises.name: Chin Tucks
exercises.focusArea: Forward head posture / deep neck flexors
exercises.notes: Activates longus colli — the muscles that atrophy from screen work. Neurological stimulus; no fatigue cost. Draw chin straight back, not down.

exercises.name: Band Pull-Aparts
exercises.notes: Highest-leverage desk-worker and climber corrective. Climbers are chronically dominant in lat/pec/biceps; this rebalances the shoulder girdle daily with near-zero cost. Keep arms straight, squeeze shoulder blades together at end range.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme A — Thoracic
mobilityRoutines.notes: Desk work drives thoracic kyphosis. Climbing locks the T-spine in flexion under load. Combined, this limits overhead reach, bench press depth, and breathing mechanics. No equipment needed — floor only.

exercises.name: Cat-Cow
exercises.duration: 10 reps slow — full end-range extension and flexion
exercises.notes: Emphasise the full arch (extension) as much as the curl. Most people underdo the extension end.

exercises.name: Thread-the-Needle
exercises.duration: 5 reps/side — 3 sec hold at end range
exercises.notes: From quadruped. Thread one arm under the body and rotate. Use the exhale to sink deeper. Keep hips level.

exercises.name: Quadruped T-Spine Rotation (hand behind head)
exercises.notes: From quadruped, one hand behind head. Rotate elbow up toward ceiling. Keep lower back still — movement comes from mid-back only.

exercises.name: Book Opener (side-lying)
exercises.duration: 5 reps/side — 5 sec hold at end range
exercises.notes: Side-lying, knees stacked at 90°. Sweep top arm across and open chest toward ceiling. Breathe into the stretch. Let gravity do the work.

exercises.name: Prone Cobra Hold
exercises.notes: Face down, arms at sides. Lift chest using mid-back — not lower back. Squeeze shoulder blades down and back. Progress from elbows bent to arms straight.

exercises.name: Seated T-Spine Rotation over Bench Back
exercises.notes: Sit astride a bench facing the back. Rotate and hook elbows on the bench back. Adds a mechanical fulcrum that isolates T-spine better than floor rotations.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme C — Shoulder & Posterior Capsule
mobilityRoutines.notes: Climbing is relentlessly internally-rotating. High pressing volume compounds this. Tight posterior capsule (GIRD) is the primary driver of shoulder impingement and rotator cuff pathology in climbers. This is the most injury-prevention critical theme. Equipment: resistance band, pull-up bar.

exercises.name: Sleeper Stretch
exercises.notes: Side-lying, bottom arm at 90° shoulder/elbow. Use top hand to gently push forearm toward floor. This is the most direct treatment for GIRD — do not skip. Stop at pain; discomfort is expected.

exercises.name: Cross-Body Shoulder Stretch
exercises.notes: Pull the arm across the chest using the opposite hand at the elbow. Keep shoulder packed down — do not let it shrug up. Complements sleeper stretch with a different capsular angle.

exercises.name: Band External Rotation (elbow at side, 90°)
exercises.focusArea: Rotator cuff ER strength — short range
exercises.notes: Band anchored to side. Elbow pinned to hip. Rotate forearm out slowly (2 sec), resist return (2 sec). This is strengthening, not stretching — control the negative.

exercises.name: Band External Rotation at 90° Abduction
exercises.focusArea: Rotator cuff ER strength — end range (most deficient in climbers)
exercises.notes: Arm out to side at shoulder height. Band from below. Rotate forearm from pointing down to pointing up. This late-range ER position is where climbers have the most deficit and highest injury risk.

exercises.name: Dead Hang
exercises.notes: Full passive hang, allow shoulders to rise toward ears. Then actively depress scapulae (pull shoulders away from ears) — hold 5 sec. Alternate between passive and active. Decompresses the joint and trains scapular retraction under load.

exercises.name: Wall Slide with Posterior Tilt
exercises.focusArea: Lower trap + serratus anterior in overhead position
exercises.notes: Hands on wall at shoulder height. Slide arms up while maintaining light contact. Keep ribs down (posterior pelvic tilt equivalent for the thorax). This trains the muscles that stabilise the scapula overhead — the primary failure point in shoulder impingement.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme D — Posterior Chain & Hamstrings
mobilityRoutines.notes: Heavy deadlifts and RDLs at 80–90%+ demand full posterior chain length-tension. Desk sitting means hamstrings never get loaded at length. This theme targets the length-tension relationship under load, not just passive flexibility. Equipment: light dumbbell (5–15 lb), bench.

exercises.name: Supine Hamstring Stretch (band or strap)
exercises.notes: Loop a band around the foot. Straighten the leg toward the ceiling. Do not force — let the weight of the leg do the work over 90 seconds. Establish the passive range before loading it.

exercises.name: Active Straight Leg Raise
exercises.focusArea: Nervous system buy-in for hamstring length
exercises.notes: Supine, one leg flat. Kick the other leg up as high as possible using hip flexors — no momentum. Hold 2 sec at top. The contrast between passive range (previous exercise) and active range reveals where the nervous system is restricting you.

exercises.name: Jefferson Curl (5–15 lb DB)
exercises.duration: 5 reps slow — 3 sec down, 1 sec hold, 3 sec up
exercises.focusArea: Full posterior chain under spinal flexion load
exercises.notes: Stand on a step or bench edge. Hold a light DB. Curl down vertebra by vertebra into full spinal flexion, reaching toward the floor. Do not rush. This builds posterior chain strength through full range — including ranges that conventional deadlifts avoid.

exercises.name: Single-Leg RDL with Pause (light DB or bodyweight)
exercises.focusArea: Eccentric hamstring strengthening + hip hinge pattern
exercises.notes: Hinge at hip, send the free leg back. Pause 2 sec at the bottom in maximum hip flexion. Stand by squeezing the glute of the working leg. The pause removes momentum and maximises time under tension at end range.

exercises.name: Nordic Hamstring Curl (feet under bench)
exercises.focusArea: Eccentric hamstring strength — highest evidence for injury prevention
exercises.notes: Kneel, feet anchored under the bench. Lower the body toward the floor as slowly as possible (aim for 5+ sec). Catch yourself with hands. Pull back up using hands as needed. Highest-evidence exercise for hamstring strain prevention. Progress by increasing reps before adding sets.

exercises.name: Hip Hinge with Band (band looped over hips, anchored forward)
exercises.focusArea: Hip hinge motor pattern + posterior loading
exercises.notes: Loop a band over the hip crease, anchored to something in front. Hinge back into the band's resistance. Hold 3 sec. The band provides tactile feedback for where the hinge axis should be and teaches posterior hip loading.

mobilityRoutines.name: Theme E — Ankle Dorsiflexion & Calf
mobilityRoutines.notes: Limited ankle dorsiflexion accounts for up to 43% of variance in squat depth. Zone 2 and VO2 interval sessions create the highest cumulative ankle and calf load of the program — Achilles tendinopathy risk peaks here. Equipment: wall, step edge or bench, resistance band.

exercises.name: Ankle DF Wall Drill
exercises.notes: Toes touching the wall, knee drives toward wall keeping heel flat. Move foot back until the furthest distance where knee can touch. Measure weekly — asymmetry of >2 cm is worth addressing. Keep this as your benchmark.

exercises.name: Half-Kneeling Ankle Mobilization (banded)
exercises.notes: Loop a band around the ankle/heel. Anchor band behind you. Drive the knee forward over the toes — the band provides posterior traction on the talus, creating joint space. This addresses the bony restriction component, not just soft tissue.

exercises.name: Calf Stretch — Bent Knee (soleus bias)
exercises.notes: Against wall, foot flat, knee bent and pushed forward. Soleus is the primary dorsiflexion restrictor in most people (not gastrocnemius). If you feel this more than the straight-knee version, the soleus is your limiting factor.

exercises.name: Calf Stretch — Straight Knee (gastrocnemius bias)
exercises.notes: Against wall or on a step, heel dropped, knee locked. Target the upper calf/back of knee. Do this after the bent-knee version to hit both heads.

exercises.name: Single-Leg Eccentric Calf Raise (off step edge)
exercises.duration: 3 × 15 reps — 3 sec down, 1 sec hold, 3 sec up
exercises.focusArea: Achilles tendon health + strength in dorsiflexion
exercises.notes: Stand on one leg on a step edge. Rise up with both legs if needed; lower on one leg only (3 sec). This is the Alfredson eccentric protocol — the highest-evidence intervention for Achilles tendinopathy prevention and treatment. Also builds calf strength at end-range dorsiflexion.

exercises.name: Toe Spreading + Foot Intrinsic Activation
exercises.notes: Seated. Spread all toes as wide as possible. Then lift just the big toe while keeping the others down; then lift the four small toes while keeping the big toe down. These intrinsic muscles prevent pronation compensation when DF is limited — the chain starts at the foot.
